topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a poorly aimed stone. Subject line: "Class Update - Ms. Albright." My stomach tightened into a knot, a familiar, unpleasant clenching. I clicked it open, and the words swam before my eyes. My son, Leo, was in Mrs. Albright’s third-grade class. The same one. A slow burn started in my chest, hot and restless. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I picked up my phone, not to call anyone, but just to feel the weight of the situation pressing down. "Of course," I muttered to the empty room, the sound sharp and brittle. I stood up, pacing the length of the living room floor, my boots thudding a rhythm against the hardwood. I needed to confront this, to shake the reality of it loose.

[story 2]

I stared at the printed roster, the ink blurring slightly under the harsh overhead light. It was impossible. My child, my little Leo, sharing a classroom with the woman who had shaped his early years. A strange, cold sensation washed over me, quickly followed by a surge of sharp irritation. I slammed the paper onto the desk. The sound echoed in the quiet house. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to look out at the manicured lawn. I felt a powerful urge to dismantle everything—to prove that this coincidence, this unwelcome mirroring, meant nothing. I clenched my jaw until my teeth ached. "Fine," I whispered, the word laced with a hard edge. "Let her teach him. I don't need to care."

[story 3]

The confirmation arrived via a casual social media post, a digital slap across my composure. My hands were shaking as I read the name: Ms. Albright. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air. A simmering resentment began to bubble up, hot enough to sting my throat. I didn't cry. Crying felt too weak, too yielding. Instead, I walked straight to the kitchen and began aggressively scrubbing the countertops, the harsh soap fumes stinging my nostrils. Every swipe of the sponge was a deliberate act of resistance. I felt a fierce need to assert control over this unwelcome symmetry, to make this shared history irrelevant. I straightened up, wiping my hands on a towel, my posture rigid, a challenge radiating from every muscle in my body.
